title: Root of the neck: subclavian artery and vein
body_html:
Subclavian vein

Knowledge of the anatomy of the subclavian vein is required by the college and useful clinically given its use for central venous access.

Origin:
  • axillary vein
  • becomes subclavian vein as it passes behind junction of 1st rib and clavicle
Termination:
  • brachiocephalic vein
  • forms brachiocephalic vein by merging with internal jugular vein behind sternoclavicular joint

Illustration showing arrangement of subclavian vessels

surface markings of subclavian vein

Subclavian artery
Origin:
  • Right: Brachiocephalic trunk behind sternoclavicular joint
  • Left: Arch of aorta
Parts:
Divided into 3 parts going from medial to lateral:
  • 1st part: from the origin at the brachiocephalic trunk on the right and arch of aorta on the left to the medial border of scalenus anterior
  • 2nd part: part posterior to scalenus anterior
  • 3rd part: part lateral border of scalenus to outer border of first rib
Termination:
  • Terminates as axillary artery at outer border of 1st rib

Palpation
  • The subclavian artery pulsation can be palpated 1 finger breadth above and parallel to the medial end of the clavicle.

Origins of the subclavian arteries

Scalenus anterior dividing subclavian artery into 3 parts

Image sourced from Wikipedia
Path of subclavian vessels in relation to scalenes
